athletic_program_information.sports_played_or_athletic_program: Varsity Boys Soccer (4 years: 2020–2024), Junior Varsity Track & Field (2 years: 2020–2022), off‑season strength and conditioning program through the BHHS athletic department.

financial_need_and_medical_details.description_of_need_for_financial_assist:
I am requesting financial assistance to help cover the costs of my first year at Santa Monica College, where I have been accepted for Fall 2025 with the goal of completing my general education and transferring to a four-year university to study kinesiology and sports medicine. My family’s financial situation is very limited, and without outside support like this scholarship, it will be extremely difficult for me to pay for tuition, books, transportation, and required athletic-related expenses.

My mother is a single parent who works full-time as a medical assistant at a small clinic in West Los Angeles. She earns a modest hourly wage, and her schedule is often unpredictable. My father has not been consistently present in my life and does not provide regular financial support; child-support payments have been inconsistent for years, and when they do arrive, they tend to go immediately to rent and utilities. We live in a small two-bedroom apartment near Beverly Drive, and my younger sister and I share one of the bedrooms to keep our housing costs as low as possible. Even with this, rent has increased twice in the last three years, and my mom has taken on extra shifts just to keep us current.

Throughout high school, I have tried to contribute as much as I can to the household while still staying committed to my academics and athletic teams. During the summers and on weekends in the off-season, I worked part-time at a local grocery store stocking shelves and assisting customers. Most of the money I earned went toward groceries and helping my mom with our phone bill and bus passes. I have very limited savings—less than $600 at the moment—because unexpected costs, like our car needing brakes last year, have always come up and taken priority.

As I prepare for college, my biggest expected expenses include community college enrollment fees, textbooks and online access codes (which can easily add up to several hundred dollars per semester), a laptop that can handle online coursework, and transportation. I plan to commute from home to Santa Monica College by bus and occasionally by rideshare when practices or late labs make public transit difficult. I do not own a car personally, and our family car is old and unreliable, so I cannot depend on it for daily transportation.

In addition to academic costs, I hope to stay involved in athletics through the college’s club soccer team and potentially as a student athletic trainer. There are fees for participation, including equipment (cleats, shin guards, training shoes), practice gear, and sometimes travel for away scrimmages or tournaments. During high school, our soccer program and BHAAA alumni support helped cover some uniform and travel costs, but in college I will be more responsible for those expenses myself. I have noticed that when I stay active in structured athletics, my grades and mental health both improve, so being able to afford this involvement is genuinely important to my success.

The funds from this scholarship would be used in a very specific and practical way. I have estimated that my first semester at Santa Monica College will cost approximately:

- Around $650 for enrollment fees and student services fees
- $400–$500 for textbooks and online course materials
- $150–$200 for a used laptop upgrade or repairs to my current one (which frequently freezes and has battery issues)
- $120 for a three-month bus pass to commute
- $200–$300 for basic soccer equipment and club team fees

Any remaining funds would be saved in a dedicated account for emergency expenses related to school (such as replacing a lost textbook or paying for a required lab fee). I am committed to continuing to work part-time while attending college, but I know from watching my mom that working too many hours can affect health and performance. This scholarship would reduce the number of hours I need to work and allow me to focus on my classes, particularly science prerequisites like biology and anatomy that will be crucial for a future in sports medicine.

I want to emphasize that athletics has been central to my life and personal growth. Playing varsity soccer at Beverly Hills High School taught me discipline, leadership, and resilience. I served as team captain my senior year, organized off-season training sessions, and mentored younger players who were struggling academically. I am the first in my family to pursue college seriously, and my coaches and teammates have been a huge support system, encouraging me to see that I can turn my passion for sports into a career helping athletes recover from injuries and stay healthy. This scholarship wouldn’t just pay bills; it would directly support my ability to continue along that path and eventually give back to the same community by working with student-athletes like the ones who inspired me.

In summary, I am asking for this assistance because my family does not have the financial resources to cover my educational and related athletic expenses without taking on unmanageable debt or sacrificing basic needs. I have worked hard in school and on the field, I plan to keep working during college, and I will use these funds carefully and responsibly. This support would make a real and immediate difference in whether I can start college strong and stay consistently involved in athletics, rather than having to step back because of money.
financial_need_and_medical_details.details_if_funds_related_to_medical_bill:
While the primary purpose of the requested funds is to support my college and athletic-related expenses, there is also an indirect medical component to my situation that has affected our family’s finances and my ability to save for school.

My mother was diagnosed with hypertension and Type 2 diabetes three years ago. She has been managing both conditions with medication, regular doctor visits, and lifestyle changes, but even with insurance through her job, there are co-pays and prescription costs that add up every month. On average, she pays around $120–$150 per month out of pocket for her medications and follow-up appointments. When her blood pressure spiked last fall, she had to go to the emergency room, which resulted in an additional bill of several hundred dollars after insurance. We are still making monthly payments on that balance.

Because of these medical expenses, any extra money we might have been able to set aside for my college has instead gone toward keeping my mom healthy and making sure she can keep working. There have been months when we had to choose between paying down the medical bill more aggressively or covering other essentials like car repairs or school-related costs for my sister and me. In those moments, my mom has always put our immediate needs first, but it has meant that there is no real college fund for me to draw from.

On a more personal level, I experienced a moderate ankle sprain during my junior year soccer season. While it did not require surgery, I needed several physical therapy sessions to recover fully and avoid long-term issues. Our insurance covered part of it, but we still had co-pays for each visit. I remember my mom hesitating before approving the therapy because she was worried about the cost, but my coach and athletic trainer strongly recommended it to prevent more serious problems in the future. We ultimately decided to proceed, and I’m grateful we did because it allowed me to return to play and avoid chronic pain. However, those co-pays were another strain on our tight budget.

I am not asking for this scholarship primarily to pay medical bills, but I think it is important to explain how ongoing health-related costs in our household have limited our ability to save for my education. The reality is that when someone in the family has chronic conditions, there are always unplanned expenses—an extra lab test, a prescription change, a follow-up appointment—that take priority over things like college savings. Receiving this scholarship would help offset the financial pressure created by these medical obligations and give me a more stable foundation as I start college.

In the long term, my goal of studying kinesiology and sports medicine is actually inspired by these experiences. I have seen firsthand how important proper treatment and prevention are, both for my mom’s chronic conditions and for my own sports injury. I hope one day to work as an athletic trainer or physical therapist, possibly even at a high school like Beverly Hills, so I can help student-athletes avoid serious injuries and stay healthy enough to pursue their goals. In that sense, the medical challenges my family has faced are part of the reason I am on this path, and this scholarship would help me move forward despite the financial obstacles they have created.
